leaders.name: Cesar Chavez
leaders.bio: Cesar Chavez was one of the main leaders of the Chicano movement and was the president of the NFWA until his death in 1993. His goal was to unionize farm workers and help give Mexican Americans the civil rights that they deserved by using nonviolent tactics. Throughout the movement, he organized strikes, protested, and helped the farm laborers register to vote. In fact, a research guide from the Library of Congress mentions how on February 14th, 1968, “César Chávez begins to fast in protest of farmworker exploitations” (Library of Congress).
leaders.quote: “Farm workers are not agricultural implements; they are not beasts of burden to be used and discarded.”

leaders.name: Dolores Huerta
leaders.bio: Dolores Huerta was a key civil rights activist and feminist during the Chicano movement. She broke down gender barriers and impacted many young women. Inspired by her mother Alicia, Huerta showed kindness and compassion to others, and she worked hard to fight for justice for Mexican Americans and women. Alongside Chavez, Huerta often protested against poor labor conditions and economic inequality, but she mainly challenged gender discrimination during the Chicano movement and labor movements (Dolores Huerta Foundation).

leaders.name: Rosalio Muñoz
leaders.bio: Rosalio Muñoz was a well-known protestor and activist during the Chicano movement. With his organization (Chicano Moratorium), he tried to protest and get other people to protest against the draft in the Vietnam War, being anti-war. His protests had a lot of people and were often interrupted by violent police actions and gunfights, leading to the deaths of notable people like Ruben Salazar. Because of his protests, FBI undercover agents monitored him for terrorist activities and forcibly removed him in 1970, but he returned soon after to run the Moratorium again (Kratz).
